About Crain & Wooley

Crain & Wooley, with their offices in Plano and Mansfield, serves the entire Dallas-Fort Worth Metro area with exemplary estate planning services. With more than 15 years of experience in crafting comprehensive estate plans, Crain & Wooley works with you to formulate the perfect plan, thereby achieving your goals and leaving an intentional legacy. Partners Justin Crain and Jacob Wooley dedicate themselves to transparency, integrity, and compassion in all things. We put action to the Texas Lawyer's Creed.

Hailing from Odessa and Lubbock, TX, Justin Crain is an estate planning attorney and partner at Crain & Wooley. He is a licensed attorney with the Supreme Court of Texas, has been licensed as a risk manager and an agent with the Texas Department of Insurance, and holds the associate risk manager designation with the American Institute for Chartered Property Casualty Underwriters.

Justin has been recognized by the Texas Bar College as one of the best-trained attorneys in Texas, and he is a member of the State Bar of Texas' Real Estate, Probate, and Trust Law Section, the National Elder Council, and the National Academy of Elder Law Attorneys. He is committed to providing ongoing education regarding important estate planning and financial issues.

Making his home in Mansfield, TX, Jacob Wooley graduated from Mississippi College School of Law and is licensed to practice in Arkansas and Texas. His experience with businesses and families allows him to address varied estate planning issues. Jacob's focus is helping clients develop a comprehensive plan that gives peace of mind knowing affairs are in order.

Working With Our Team

Transparency, integrity, and compassion are hallmarks of our practice. Together, we have helped hundreds of families, just like yours, achieve peace of mind by applying our experience to the confusing world of probate, wills, living trusts, irrevocable trusts, special needs trusts, Medicaid applications, and much, much more.

We fully believe in our ability to help you reach your estate planning goals, and we back our work with two guarantees:

1. No Risk, Money Back Guarantee: If, before we deliver your plan documents, you are not satisfied with the services received, simply let us know that you are not satisfied, and we will refund 100% of your costs.
2. One Business Day Call-Back Guarantee: If, before we deliver your plan documents, a firm employee does not return your phone call promptly (within one business day), we will refund your cost and complete your plan free of charge.

Fee Structure

You will never receive a surprise bill from Crain & Wooley. Our advanced planning will, trust, and Medicaid/nursing home application services are billed at a flat rate. The uncertain nature of probate, emergency Medicaid, and guardianship cases necessitates billing our reasonable hourly rate for those situations. View our flat rate and hourly service options!

Proactively completing a comprehensive estate plan is one of the greatest gifts you can give your loved ones. Comprehensive Will Plan | $2,115

The Comprehensive Will Plan offers a thorough and accessible estate planning solution for those who want to ensure that their wishes are clearly documented and that they receive the guidance they need. This plan includes the creation of a Last Will and Testament along with other essential documents to manage your affairs during your lifetime and upon your passing. Unlike our basic Will Plan, the Comprehensive Will Plan includes 12 months of access to our firm at no additional charge, allowing you to make updates and ask questions without worrying about extra fees. We also take the time to review your documents with you, ensuring that you fully understand their contents and how to use your estate plan effectively.

Comprehensive Trust Plan | $6,985

The Comprehensive Trust Portfolio is a complete, turn-key estate planning solution designed for those who want thorough, personalized guidance and peace of mind. Unlike our Will Plan and Trust Plan, which provide essential documents but do not include additional assistance or ongoing education, the Comprehensive Trust Portfolio goes beyond the basics. With this plan, you receive not only the foundational estate planning documents but also an entire year of unlimited support—without worrying about being billed for every meeting, question, or update.

What makes this plan truly unique is that all services are provided for a single, one-time flat fee. This means you won't face unexpected charges for meetings, revisions, or additional requests. We take care of everything from start to finish, including drafting and filing documents, reviewing your assets, and providing ongoing education to ensure you fully understand how your estate plan works. The typical process takes 2-3 months, but with 12 months of access, you have ample time to ask questions, adjust your plan, and gain the confidence that everything is handled correctly—without the pressure of running out of time or incurring extra costs.

By choosing the Comprehensive Trust Portfolio, you gain the assurance that your plan is not only comprehensive but also proactively managed. You receive the support you need to make sure every asset is accounted for, every document is understood, and every detail of your estate plan is implemented correctly. This inclusive approach allows you to focus on what matters most—knowing that your estate plan is ready to serve your needs and protect your legacy.

Included Documents and Services (Click Here for Legal Term Descriptions):
Durable Power of Attorney; Medical Power of Attorney; HIPAA Release; Directive to Physicians; Declaration of Guardians in Advance of Need; Pour-Over Will; Document Signing Services; Written Legal Education; Living Trust; Trust Funding Education Memo; Memorandum of Personal Property; One Texas Deed Transfer; Memorial Wishes; Scheduled Document Review and Education; Scheduled Asset Review and Trust Funding Education; Secure Client Portal & Document Storage; Unlimited Support and Updates for 12 Months; Estate Plan and Law Review (Month 10); Client-Only Webinars; Estate Plan Organizational System

Lifetime Lawyer Program | $9,970

The Lifetime Lawyer Program offers a truly comprehensive estate planning experience, designed for clients seeking ongoing support without worrying about additional costs. Whether you choose to use a Living Trust, Last Will and Testament, or a combination of both as part of your plan, our program provides lifetime assistance to ensure your estate plan remains effective and aligned with your goals. This includes continuous support not just for you, but also for those who will manage your estate after you, such as Trustees and Executors, ensuring they have the guidance they need to fulfill their roles.

With the Lifetime Lawyer Program, all services are included for as long as you are living (or, in the case of married couples, as long as one spouse is living), making it easier to navigate life changes and legal updates. You'll receive all necessary documents, unlimited updates, and ongoing legal education, allowing us to focus on building a lasting relationship with you—without the worry of being billed by the hour or for every meeting. It's a turn-key solution that handles everything from the creation and filing of documents to the education needed to ensure you fully understand your estate plan.

Key Benefits of the Lifetime Lawyer Program:
Unlimited Support and Updates for Life: Make as many changes or updates to your estate plan as needed, ask questions, and receive guidance without worrying about hourly fees. Whether you need adjustments due to life events, law changes, or simply have questions about your documents, we are here for you with no additional charges.
Annual Law Review and Unlimited Meetings: Each year, we provide you with a report detailing updates on estate and tax laws, helping you stay informed about relevant changes. If you want to understand how these updates might impact your specific estate plan, you can schedule a meeting with us anytime, at no extra cost, ensuring your plan remains aligned with your goals.
Client-Only Webinars: Access exclusive webinars that offer ongoing education on estate planning topics and updates on legal matters. At the end of each session, you'll have the opportunity to ask questions and receive personalized answers from our legal team, giving you greater clarity and confidence in your estate planning decisions.
Ongoing Support for Your Trustees and Executors: Our commitment extends beyond your lifetime. Upon your passing, your Successor Trustee or Executor will receive a complimentary one-hour consultation with our firm. This includes trust or will education, training, and a plan review, helping ensure a smooth transition and proper management of your estate according to your wishes. With the Lifetime Lawyer Program, you gain peace of mind knowing that your estate plan is supported and maintained throughout your life and beyond. Our goal is to provide you and your family with the confidence that everything is taken care of, ensuring a seamless experience for you now and a smooth transition for your heirs in the future.

Included Documents and Services (Click Here for Legal Term Descriptions):

Virtual Services FAQ

What is the first thing I should do?
Stop procrastinating and schedule a complimentary consultation to talk 1:1 with one of our attorneys.

What is a basic will?
A basic will directs for the immediate distribution of assets and contains no specific provisions such as a testamentary trust for minor children, etc.

What is a basic trust?
A basic trust directs for the immediate distribution of assets and contains no specific provisions such as divorce proofing via descendant's trusts, special needs provisions, international asset distribution, etc.

What is a comprehensive trust?
A comprehensive trust contains more complex distributions instructions including, but not limited to, such things as divorce protection via descendant's trusts, trusts for minor beneficiaries, education trusts, age appropriate distributions, generational trusts, annual distributions, charitable bequests and more.

What are some benefits of trust planning?
There are too many to list, but here are a few: trusts are portable state-to-state; trusts (when funding correctly) avoid probate; trusts keep your affairs private; trusts are flexible enough to accomplish personal goals like creating an education fund; trusts protect an inheritance from divorce; trusts plan for a loved one with special needs and much, much more!

Why do you conduct monthly legal education?
One of the keys to having an up-to-date estate plan is to keep abreast of changes and best-practices in all areas of estate and finance law. While not every month will pertain to your specific situation, you will find the information presented informative and empowering.

Texas is a community property state. Why do spouses need to write a will or trust?
Community property is vastly misunderstood in Texas. Community property means that each spouse owns 50% of asset "X" and as such needs to leave instructions on how to dispose of that 50% upon his or her death. Assets do NOT automatically transfer to a surviving spouse.

Why is the Comprehensive Trust Portfolio most popular?
The Comprehensive Trust Portfolio allows Crain & Wooley to provide you with the highest level of service without worrying about the billable hour. The first 12 months of having a trust are the most important; it is during these months that you fund your trust. This portfolio gives you access to the firm for 12 months for assistance, questions, advice, changes and more without incurring billable hour charges.

What comes in the Annual Report?
Around month 11 of Comprehensive Trust Portfolio service, we conduct a review detailing: implications of law and tax changes, beneficiaries, special beneficiary instructions, assets funded into the trust and power of attorney agents. This report acts as a reminder of how your plan works and what it accomplishes as well as gives you about 1 month to request any changes be completed free of charge.

Why should I pay a lawyer to write my will or trust when I can do it myself online for A LOT cheaper?
To be blunt, you get what you pay for. If you look closely legal self-help websites, you will see disclaimer statements like "this is not legal advice" and "services are not protected by attorney-client privilege or as legal work product". These template services don't provide you with the opportunity to engage with an attorney for comprehensive planning: current goals, future goals, what-if planning, tax planning and much more. So, the real question is why would you trust a non-legal entity to draw up documents that dispose of all your hard-earned assets?

What is a digital document locker?
Secure exchange of information is vital to crafting your perfect estate plan. Crain & Wooley utilizes SmartVault, an SEC and FNRA approved, cloud-based document storage system. When using SmartVault we can securely communicate about your assets, end of life plans and general estate information without worrying about cyber security. SmartVault also allows you to store all your vital information like life insurance contracts, estate planning documents, assets lists and more in a secure manner while being able to access the information from anywhere on the globe.

How do I know if I need a will or a trust?
Schedule a free consultation with one of our attorneys; tell us your goals and concerns and then we will outline which estate planning tool best accomplishes your current and future goals.

What is funding a trust?
Funding your trust are the steps taken to make your trust is the owner of titled assets like your house, financial accounts, CDs, etc. Don't worry – these actions do NOT impact your tax rate or your tax exemptions AND you remain in complete control of all assets. Your daily life does not change.

Does a will require probate?
Short answer – yes. There are at least 10 different types of probate. Schedule a time to talk with our attorneys to learn which type of probate fits your situation.

What happens when my first 12 months is up?
You have the option of renewing for $595/year to gain an additional 12 months of access to the firm. This access provides you with unlimited firm support for questions, changes and amendments without worrying about the billable hour. You will also receive an annual report for each renewal year.

What if I only want a will or a trust and nothing else?
Crain & Wooley does not try to up-sell or talk you into anything. We will lay out the best option for your situation and then complete only the work that you deem necessary.

Do you offer payment plan options?
Yes, our firm offers internal financing options.

What services do you offer?
Crain & Wooley is a full-service estate planning law firm. We have expertise in standard & taxable trusts, standard & complex wills, probate, adult guardianship, nursing home/Medicaid planning, small business formation, real estate transactions, existing document review/updates and more.

I am not rich. Why do I need an estate plan?
It's a common misconception that estate planning is only for the wealthy. In reality, having an estate plan is important for everyone, no matter the size of your estate. An estate plan ensures that your assets—no matter how modest—are distributed according to your wishes, providing peace of mind that your loved ones are cared for. Without a plan in place, your estate becomes subject to intestacy laws, meaning that the state decides how your assets will be divided. Intestacy can be a lengthy and costly process, often requiring heirship proceedings and the appointment of an attorney ad litem to represent unknown heirs. This can create significant delays, expenses, and stress for your loved ones, all while potentially resulting in outcomes you wouldn't have chosen—like distant relatives inheriting your assets. By creating a personalized estate plan, you maintain control over what happens to your assets, ensuring they go to the people or causes you care about most. You can avoid the complications of intestacy, simplify the process for your loved ones, and ensure that your wishes are honored. At our firm, we make estate planning accessible and straightforward, no matter your situation. Contact us today to learn how we can help you create a plan that protects your legacy and gives you peace of mind.

What technology do I need for virtual services?
For 100% virtual services, you will need a computer, tablet or smartphone with a camera and file sharing capabilities. Each document signer (husband, wife, etc.) will need a separate email address. A free email service such as Gmail or Yahoo will work just fine.

Flat Rate Estate Planning Services

Flat Rate Estate Planning Services Proactively completing a comprehensive estate plan is one of the greatest gifts you can give your loved ones. You will provide peace of mind knowing that your estate settlement will be greatly simplified and estate administration costs lessened or eliminated depending on the estate planning tool you choose to use – a Will or a Trust.

Whether you are creating a plan for the first time or updating existing documents, our team offers comprehensive estate planning services tailored to your goals. Why Choose an Estate Planning Lawyer in Allen?

Local experience is crucial when it comes to estate planning, as laws can vary significantly from one locality to another. An estate planning attorney in Allen is familiar with Texas state laws and any local regulations that could impact your estate plan. Understanding Texas Law - Texas laws on wills, trusts, and probate can be complex. Personalized Service - a lawyer in Allen ensures face-to-face interactions.

When you work with a lawyer who regularly appears in Collin County probate courts and understands how local judges and procedures operate, you gain practical guidance that goes beyond what generic forms can provide. A local estate planning lawyer can also coordinate strategies if you own property in nearby communities such as Plano, McKinney, or Frisco.

Our Allen Estate Planning Services: Comprehensive & Transparent

At Crain & Wooley, our estate planning services are designed to cover every aspect of your needs. We cater to individuals and families across diverse backgrounds and income levels. As part of our commitment to thorough service, we also evaluate potential long-term care plans and asset protection strategies tailored to your future needs.

We work with you to create and coordinate key documents such as wills, revocable living trusts, financial powers of attorney, and medical directives so that each piece of your estate plan supports the others.

Our Allen estate planning services commonly include: Foundational documents such as wills, revocable living trusts, and powers of attorney to address incapacity and asset transfers; Healthcare planning with medical powers of attorney and advance directives; Long-term care strategies that consider future needs, potential Medicaid eligibility, and how to protect key assets when appropriate; Business and tax planning for owners and professionals who need to address succession, buy-sell arrangements, or tax-efficient transfers.

FAQs About Estate Planning in Allen

What Is a First Step in Estate Planning? The first step in estate planning is to evaluate your current assets and clarify your goals. It's essential to review your financials, including your properties, accounts, and any other significant assets. During an initial meeting, we typically walk through a detailed questionnaire that covers your family structure, existing documents, and concerns.

Why Do I Need an Estate Planning Attorney in Allen? An estate planning attorney can provide tailored advice and help create a legally sound estate plan. Local attorneys understand Texas-specific rules, which can affect the distribution of your assets. An estate planning lawyer in Allen can explain how the probate process works in Collin County.

How Often Should I Update My Estate Plan? Your estate plan should be reviewed at significant life events, such as marriages, births, or a change in financial circumstances. Generally, we recommend reviewing your estate plan every three to five years.

What Happens If I Don't Have an Estate Plan? If you pass away without an estate plan in Allen, your assets will be distributed according to Texas intestacy laws. This typically means that assets go to the closest family members, which may not align with your wishes. Without a plan, your estate could face lengthy and costly probate processes. An estate plan also specifies your healthcare directives.

How We Work With Estate Planning Clients

When you reach out to Crain & Wooley, we begin by listening carefully to your concerns and goals. From there, we outline a clear roadmap. We typically start with an initial consultation, which can take place in our office or virtually. After that meeting, we work on tailored recommendations and draft documents that reflect Texas law and the realities of administering estates in local courts such as those in Collin County.

During the planning process, you can expect: Clear communication about timelines, next steps, and what we need from you; Organized document preparation that brings together wills, trusts, and powers of attorney; Thoughtful discussion of how your choices may affect your family members, beneficiaries, and chosen decision makers.

Take the Next Step: Secure Your Peace of Mind Today

At Crain & Wooley, we prioritize transparency, integrity, and compassion in estate planning. Don't wait until it's too late—ensure that your future and loved ones are protected. Contact us today to schedule a consultation with an estate planning attorney in Allen.

/allen/asset-protection.body:
At Crain & Wooley, we understand the importance of safeguarding your assets for future generations. Whether you're concerned about shielding your finances from creditors or ensuring seamless wealth transfer, our asset protection strategies provide peace of mind.

Asset protection isn't just about shielding wealth—it's about ensuring your life's work is preserved for your family and future endeavors. In Allen, TX, our understanding of state-specific laws allows us to tailor protection strategies that align perfectly with local regulations. For instance, Texas homestead laws can offer significant exemptions, making it crucial to incorporate such nuances into your plan.

Additionally, the complexity of asset protection often calls for diverse strategies such as trusts, limited liability companies, and family limited partnerships. These instruments must be structured meticulously to maximize protective benefits. At Crain & Wooley, we offer a sophisticated blend of legal structures, applying them judiciously to counter potential risks.

Our Asset Protection Process

Initial Consultation: We start with a detailed discussion to understand your unique financial situation and goals. Customized Strategy: Our team crafts a personalized asset protection plan tailored to your specific needs. Implementation: We assist in setting up legal instruments like trusts and LLCs to shield your assets effectively.

Once your initial consultation is complete, we move towards a collaborative strategy phase. We customize every plan component to align with your life goals and potential challenges identified during our evaluations.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is Asset Protection? Asset protection involves legal strategies designed to safeguard one's wealth from potential creditors and lawsuits. It often includes the use of trusts, business entities, and other legal entities to shield personal finances from loss or liquidation.

What Steps Should I Take to Protect My Assets? To start protecting your assets, consider forming legal structures like trusts or family limited partnerships, which can separate personal wealth from business liabilities. It's also important to regularly review and update these strategies.

Can Asset Protection Protect Me from All Creditors? While asset protection techniques can significantly reduce risk exposure, they cannot eliminate it entirely. Certain legal obligations, such as tax liabilities or fraud claims, may still pose a risk.

Secure Your Future with Confidence

At Crain & Wooley, we offer dedicated support and innovative asset protection solutions that address your financial concerns with precision and care.

/allen/guardianship.body:
At Crain & Wooley, we understand the complexities of establishing guardianship in Allen, TX, and are dedicated to providing compassionate and personalized legal services to guide you through this sensitive process. Our team of experienced guardianship attorneys is committed to helping you protect the best interests of your loved ones. In Allen, where local regulations and community norms play a significant role in guardianship cases, having knowledgeable support from a seasoned guardianship lawyer is crucial.

Our Comprehensive Guardianship Services

Guardianship proceedings can be emotional and challenging experiences. Our services include: Understanding Client Needs - through in-depth consultations, we focus on understanding your unique situation, whether you are dealing with a minor or an incapacitated adult. Legal Representation in Court - we provide robust representation in Allen courts to secure guardianships for minors or incapacitated adults, with familiarity with the Collin County Courthouse. Comprehensive Paperwork & Filings - our team meticulously handles all documentation to prevent issues and expedite the process.

Guardianship Process in Allen, TX: Step-by-Step

Initial Consultation: We start with a personalized consultation to understand your needs and explain the guardianship process specific to Allen, including requirements set by the Texas Estates Code. Filing the Application: We assist you in preparing and filing a guardianship application at the Collin County Courthouse. Appointment of Guardian ad Litem: The court may appoint a guardian ad litem to evaluate the situation. Court Hearing: We represent you during the court hearing, advocating for the appointment of a guardian that's in your loved one's best interests. Post-Appointment Support: Beyond the initial proceedings, we provide ongoing support for fulfilling court-mandated duties.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is Guardianship? Guardianship is a legal arrangement where a court-appointed individual is granted the authority to make personal and/or financial decisions for another person, referred to as the ward. This arrangement is typically necessary when an individual, such as a minor or an incapacitated adult, cannot manage their own affairs.

Who Can Be a Guardian? In Allen, Texas, a guardian can be any competent adult who the court determines is suitable to oversee the ward's personal and/or financial affairs. While parents or relatives are commonly appointed, the court's priority is the well-being of the ward.

What Are the Responsibilities of a Guardian? Typically, duties include managing the ward's assets, making healthcare or educational decisions, and ensuring the ward's overall well-being. Guardians must provide annual reports to the court.

How Long Does the Guardianship Process Take? The timeline can vary based on the complexity of the case and court schedules. Generally, the process can take several months from the filing of the application to the final court hearing.

Can Guardianship Be Contested? Yes, guardianship can be contested by interested parties. Common grounds for contesting include the suitability of the proposed guardian or the necessity of the guardianship itself.

Take the Next Step to Secure Guardianship with Confidence

If you're facing the complex guardianship process, let Crain & Wooley guide you with experience and care. With our transparent pricing and commitment to client satisfaction, you can proceed with confidence and peace of mind knowing your loved ones are in capable hands.

Asset inventory and management: We support the executor or administrator in identifying, valuing, and responsibly managing the estate's assets. 5. Distribution and closing: Upon court approval, we supervise the fair and legal distribution of funds and property and finalize the case.

Types Of Texas Probate And Alternatives For Allen Families

Not every estate in Texas follows the same path. Some families are best served by a full independent administration, where the court grants authority to the executor with limited ongoing supervision. Others may qualify for a more streamlined process based on the size of the estate or the way assets are titled.

Certain smaller estates may be able to use tools like a small estate affidavit or an affidavit of heirship when specific conditions in the Texas Estates Code are met. When there is a well-drafted will and cooperative heirs, an independent administration is often the most efficient option. In contrast, if there are major disputes or questions about the validity of a will, the court may require a dependent administration with greater oversight.

Frequently Asked Questions

How Long Does Probate Usually Take in Allen? Probate varies based on the size and complexity of the estate. Some straightforward cases may conclude within a few months, while estates involving multiple properties or family disputes could take a year or more.

What Does an Executor or Administrator Actually Do? An executor or administrator is responsible for gathering the deceased's assets, notifying creditors, resolving outstanding debts or taxes, and ensuring property is distributed to the rightful heirs or beneficiaries.

Can Probate Be Handled Without a Lawyer? While smaller or uncontested estates can sometimes be managed without legal representation, Texas probate law is detailed and sometimes strict. For families with complicated assets, business interests, or potential conflicts, partnering with a probate attorney helps prevent mistakes.

Are Probate & Estate Administration the Same Thing? Probate is the legal process of validating a will and overseeing distribution with court supervision, while estate administration refers more broadly to managing and closing out a person's assets after they pass away. Most estate cases will involve both elements.

If you care for a child or adult with disabilities, you probably think about their future every day. You want them to have resources for comfort and opportunity, yet you also know that a direct gift or inheritance can disrupt vital SSI or Medicaid benefits. That tension is exactly why many families look for a pooled special needs trusts attorney in Allen.

At Crain & Wooley, we help families in Allen and across the Dallas–Fort Worth area use tools such as pooled special needs trusts to protect both government benefits and quality of life.

What Pooled Special Needs Trusts Do

A pooled special needs trust is a type of trust managed by a nonprofit organization that combines, or pools, the funds of many beneficiaries for investment while maintaining separate sub-accounts for each person. This structure is permitted under federal law and works alongside Texas benefit rules.

When funds are placed in a properly structured pooled trust account, they are generally not treated as countable resources for means-tested programs, such as SSI and Medicaid. The organization that runs the trust acts as trustee and uses funds to pay for approved expenses that improve the beneficiary's quality of life. Common examples include education, therapies, technology, or travel.

For families in and around Allen, a pooled trust can be helpful when there is no individual available to serve as trustee or when the amount involved makes a private trust less practical. An individual special needs trust gives a family more control over trustee choice and custom terms, but it usually requires more administration and higher costs. A pooled trust offers administration by an established nonprofit, with standardized rules.

Is A Pooled Trust Right For You

The answer usually depends on the size and source of the funds, your loved one's current benefits, and who is available to help manage money in the long run. A pooled trust can make sense when the amount available is modest in relation to the cost of running a private trust, when you prefer an experienced nonprofit trustee, or when you want a ready-made structure.

Leaving assets directly to a person with disabilities can cause them to lose or be denied SSI or Medicaid until funds are spent down. Trying to solve the problem by leaving money to another relative for safekeeping can create its own risks, including divorce, creditors, or disagreements.

You may want to discuss a pooled special needs trust if: You have a loved one who receives or may receive SSI or Medicaid; You expect them to inherit funds, receive a settlement, or be named on life insurance; You are not comfortable naming a relative or friend as long term trustee; You prefer a standardized, nonprofit trustee structure; You want to coordinate planning with other parts of your Texas estate plan.

How Our Attorneys Support Your Plan In Allen

The process generally begins with an initial meeting, held either at our Plano office or virtually. We listen to your concerns, review your loved one's current benefits, and learn about the assets that need protection. If a pooled trust is appropriate, we coordinate the legal documents that work with the pooled trust agreement that the nonprofit organization provides.

Our flat-rate pricing for proactive planning means you know in advance what our work will cost. We offer a lifetime guarantee option that allows your documents to be reviewed and updated over time. We commit to timely responses on important client matters and support that commitment with a money-back guarantee tied to responsiveness.

Coordinating Your Texas Estate Plan

A pooled special needs trust is usually one piece of a broader Texas estate plan. That includes your will, any revocable or irrevocable trusts, powers of attorney, advance directives, and beneficiary designations. We pay close attention to retirement accounts and life insurance policies, which often represent significant value. Our attorneys are members of the Texas Bar College, ElderCounsel, and the National Academy of Elder Law Attorneys.

Frequently Asked Questions

How does a pooled special needs trust affect SSI and Medicaid? A properly structured pooled special needs trust is generally designed so that funds in the sub account do not count as resources for SSI or Medicaid. The nonprofit trustee pays for approved expenses directly.

What will it cost to set up a pooled trust with your firm? We use flat-rate pricing for proactive planning work rather than hourly billing. During your consultation, we quote a clear fee that covers design meetings, document drafting, and signing.

We discuss your goals, your loved one's needs, and the size and source of funds, then talk through features of different pooled trust providers. While you select the organization, we explain how their documents work with your estate plan.

Can you update our plan if laws or benefits change? Yes. We offer a lifetime guarantee option that allows for ongoing review and updates of your planning documents.

How soon should I start planning for my child's future? It is usually wise to begin planning as soon as you know your loved one may rely on needs-based benefits or long term support. Early planning gives you more options.

Understanding the Importance of Trusts in Allen

Understanding the intricacies of trusts can be pivotal for estate planning. Trusts are legal arrangements where one party holds property for the benefit of another. They are crucial tools to ensure your assets are managed and distributed according to your wishes, providing peace of mind for you and your loved ones.

By working with a trust lawyer in Allen, you can navigate Texas state laws that govern these arrangements, ensuring compliance and effectiveness. Trusts can protect your assets from probate, minimize estate taxes, and provide privacy regarding financial matters. Our team at Crain & Wooley is well-versed in creating personalized plans that cater to the unique needs of our community.

Trusts are also an important part of incapacity planning. If you are ever unable to manage your financial affairs, a properly structured trust allows your chosen trustee to step in without court intervention. This proactive approach can help prevent disruption and preserve stability for your loved ones.

For families in Allen, trusts are often used to coordinate real estate, retirement accounts, business interests, and investment portfolios into a unified plan. When properly integrated, a trust can reduce confusion for beneficiaries and streamline administration if your loved ones ever need to work through the Allen probate process.

To speak with a trust attorney about your options, call (972) 945-1610 or contact our office to schedule a consultation.

Types of Trusts in Allen & Their Benefits

Choosing the right type of trust depends on individual goals and circumstances. With our guidance, clients can select from various options, each offering unique advantages:

Revocable living trusts: Allow you to maintain control over your assets while alive and provide a seamless transfer to beneficiaries upon your passing.

Irrevocable trusts: These offer asset protection, potentially reducing estate taxes and shielding assets from creditors.

Including specific trusts like special needs trusts can also ensure that loved ones with disabilities receive necessary support without jeopardizing their eligibility for government benefits. These tailored trusts can provide a sustainable financial plan for those who require ongoing care.

Common Questions About Trusts

What Is a Trust & How Does It Work?

A trust is a legal entity created through which a person (the trustor) gives another party (the trustee) the right to hold assets for the benefit of a third party (the beneficiary). Trusts can be instrumental in estate planning, ensuring that assets are distributed efficiently and according to the trustor's wishes. Different types of trusts serve various purposes, from managing estate taxes to protecting assets from creditors.

How Can Trusts Provide Tax Benefits?

Trusts can offer considerable tax advantages by potentially reducing estate taxes and, in some cases, providing income tax benefits. Irrevocable trusts, for example, remove assets from the trustor's taxable estate, which can considerably decrease estate taxes. It is critical to consult with a trust lawyer in Allen to understand the tax implications and ensure that your trust is structured optimally for your specific situation.

Beyond tax reduction, trusts can enable strategic gift planning, allowing you to distribute assets in a tax-efficient manner during your lifetime. Understanding the nuances of these strategies can greatly enhance the financial legacy you leave behind.

What Is the Difference Between a Trust & a Will?

Both trusts and wills are fundamental components of estate planning but serve distinct purposes. A will directs how your assets will be distributed upon your passing and names guardians for minors. A trust, meanwhile, manages assets during and after your lifetime, allowing for more detailed control over how and when your estate is distributed. Trusts also bypass probate, leading to quicker distribution to beneficiaries.

How Do I Choose the Right Trustee?

Choosing the right trustee is crucial as they will be responsible for managing the trust assets and ensuring they are distributed according to the trustor's wishes. Many people choose family members, close friends, or professional fiduciaries as trustees. It's important to select someone you trust, who has strong financial acumen and is capable of managing the responsibilities of a trustee.

Furthermore, considering a corporate trustee can provide professional management and unbiased decision-making, which may be advantageous for complex estates. We can guide you through this decision, ensuring a trustee is chosen who aligns perfectly with your estate objectives.

What Should I Expect When Setting Up a Trust with Crain & Wooley?

When setting up a trust with Crain & Wooley, you can expect a comprehensive and personalized approach. Our team will walk you through every step, from understanding your goals to drafting documents and executing the trust. We aim to provide clarity and comfort, ensuring your affairs are in order. Additionally, you can expect ongoing support and annual reviews of your trust to ensure it continues to align with your evolving needs and changing legislation. This proactive approach guarantees that your estate plan remains current and effective.

For residents of Allen and nearby communities, we also explain how your trust fits within the broader framework of Texas estate laws and local court procedures. As your trust attorney, we discuss practical steps such as funding your trust, organizing your records, and communicating your plan to family members so they know how to work with your trustee if the Collin County probate court ever needs to be involved. By addressing these details in advance, we help you move from uncertainty to a clear, actionable plan.

Funding and Maintaining Your Trust in Allen

Once your documents are signed, the next step is making sure your trust actually owns or controls the right assets. Many plans fall short because the trust was never properly funded, leaving families to navigate the probate process in the Collin County courts even though they believed everything was taken care of. We walk you through which bank accounts, real estate, and other assets should be retitled or assigned so that your written plan works the way you intended.

Over time, you may open new accounts, buy or sell property, or change how your retirement benefits are structured, and these changes can affect how well your trust functions. As a trust attorney Allen families can turn to for ongoing guidance, we encourage periodic check-ins to confirm that new assets are addressed and that beneficiary designations still match your wishes. This ongoing attention helps keep your plan aligned with changes in your life and in Texas law, so your trust continues to protect your family when it matters most.

Protecting Your Child's Inheritance With Clear Planning

Leaving an inheritance to a minor child without a proper legal structure guarantees court intervention and unnecessary financial risk. Under the law, minors cannot directly own or manage significant assets like life insurance payouts, real estate, or cash inheritances. Without a specific trust in place, a judge in Allen will step in to appoint a financial guardian, which drains the estate through ongoing court fees and ultimately hands the entire unrestricted lump sum to an unprepared eighteen-year-old.

At Crain & Wooley, we help parents and guardians in Allen and across Collin County create minor trusts that reflect their values and goals. Our attorneys focus on estate planning and related fields, and we take time to explain how each decision affects your child's future. We work to make the process straightforward and manageable, even if you are starting from scratch.

Call (972) 945-1610 to schedule a consultation with our minor trust attorneys in Allen today to protect your child's inheritance and establish a secure financial future.

Why Allen Families Use Minor Trusts

Many parents assume that naming their children in a will is enough. In Texas, when a minor inherits directly, a court often needs to appoint someone to manage the child's money. This process can involve extra time, expense, and limits on how funds are used. At age 18, the child may receive what is left with few restrictions.

Families in Allen often use minor trusts to avoid these outcomes. A minor trust lets you decide who will manage assets for your child, what expenses those assets can cover, and when your child may receive money outright. You can include support for day-to-day needs, education costs, and health care, all under clear instructions for a trusted adult.

Parents also worry about family conflict if something happens to them. A well drafted minor trust gives guidance to relatives and reduces guesswork, which can lessen disputes. Our goal is to help you design a structure that gives children what they need at the right times, while preserving savings for long-term stability.

Because we work with Texas estate planning rules every day, we understand how courts in Collin County generally treat inheritances left to minors. We take that knowledge and apply it to your specific situation so your trust documents are aligned with state law and with the realities of raising children here in the Dallas–Fort Worth area.

How We Design Minor Trust Plans

When you work with our team to create a minor trust, we follow a structured yet flexible process. We start with a consultation, in person or virtual, where we learn about your family, your children, and the assets that might pass to them. We ask about your priorities, such as paying for college, supporting a child with unique needs, or encouraging savings habits.

Next, we guide you through the key choices involved in your plan. We discuss who should serve as trustee, what authority that person will have, and how distributions should work over time. We help you think through different ages or milestones for giving your child greater access to funds, and how to handle situations where a trustee can use discretion for your child's benefit.

During our planning meetings, we help you decide:

Who should manage money for your child if you are no longer able to do so

What expenses the trust may pay, such as education, medical care, and basic support

How and when your child can receive larger distributions or full control of funds

How to address younger children, future children, or blended family situations

Once we understand your decisions, we draft the trust language and any related estate planning documents, often including wills and powers of attorney, so your plan works as a whole. We review the documents with you in plain language, answer questions, and make revisions if needed. Our flat-rate pricing for proactive planning covers this design and review work, so you know your cost ahead of time and do not have to worry about hourly bills for each question.

Over time, children grow, assets change, and your views may shift. To address this, we offer a lifetime guarantee option that allows your documents to be reviewed and updated under the terms of that program. Many parents in Allen find comfort in knowing their minor trust can evolve as their children move from elementary school to high school, college, and beyond.

Working With Our Minor Trust Lawyer Allen Team

For many parents, a major concern is how much time and effort this planning will require. Our role is to make the process as efficient and comfortable as possible. You can meet with us at our Plano office, which serves many Allen families, or choose a virtual meeting if that better fits your schedule. Either way, we structure meetings to respect your time and provide clear takeaways.

During the first meeting, we talk through your children, your financial picture, and your current documents, if you have any. There is no expectation that you arrive with everything perfectly organized. We help you identify what information is most important and explain how a minor trust could coordinate with existing life insurance, retirement accounts, and real estate in Collin County or elsewhere.

Education is a central part of how we work. We do not simply hand you a stack of paperwork. Instead, we walk through key provisions in your minor trust, explain how they operate under Texas law, and answer questions in everyday language. We also provide resources, such as written summaries or educational materials, so you can discuss decisions with a spouse or other family members outside the meeting.

Communication often makes or breaks a planning experience. Parents in Allen who are juggling work, school schedules, and caregiving frequently tell us that knowing they can get timely answers reduces their stress about moving forward with a plan.

Our affiliations with Texas Bar College, ElderCounsel, and the National Academy of Elder Law Attorneys reflect our ongoing training and our focus on estate and elder law topics that often overlap with minor trusts. Combined with our local presence in the Dallas–Fort Worth area, these connections help us stay current on developments that may affect your child's plan.

Is A Minor Trust Right For You

Not every family has the same planning needs, but there are common situations where a minor trust is especially helpful. If you have life insurance that would pay a significant benefit if you pass away, own a home in or around Allen, or hold retirement accounts that name your children as backup beneficiaries, a minor trust can provide structure for those funds. It is often more secure than leaving assets outright to a child or relying on an informal understanding among relatives.

We also see parents who created a basic will years ago that simply leaves everything to their children. On paper, this may look simple, but in practice it can lead to court involvement and to a large lump sum landing in a young adult's hands at 18. A minor trust gives you the option to stretch support over time, match distributions with maturity, and protect savings intended for college or other important goals.

As you consider whether a minor trust fits, it may help to reflect on a few questions. Are you comfortable with your child having full control of inherited funds as soon as they become a legal adult, or would you prefer a gradual handoff? Do you have a person you trust to manage money in a careful, consistent way for your child's benefit? Would you like your plan to cover both everyday needs and longer-term investments, such as tuition or a first home purchase?

These are the types of issues we talk through with families in Allen. In a consultation, we can review your current documents and beneficiary designations, then give you a tailored recommendation. Our goal is not to push you toward a particular answer, but to help you understand your options so you can make decisions that fit your family and your budget.

Frequently Asked Questions

Do I really need a minor trust if I already have a will?

A will is important, but it may not control how assets are managed for a minor. Courts often appoint someone to hold funds, and your child can typically receive what remains at 18. A minor trust can align management and timing with your wishes.

Can I change the trustee or terms of the trust later?

In many cases, parents can change trustees or adjust terms while they are living and have capacity. We explain your options when we design the plan. Our lifetime guarantee option is available to help keep documents updated as family and financial situations change.

How long does the minor trust planning process take?

For most families, we can move from initial meeting to signed documents within a few weeks, depending on your schedule and how quickly decisions are made. We keep the process organized and communicate clearly so you always know the next step.

What should I prepare before meeting your team?

It helps to bring a list of your children, your major assets, and any existing estate documents. You do not need every detail sorted out. We guide you through the key questions and provide education so you can make informed decisions at your own pace.

Our trust litigation services are designed to cover every aspect of potential disputes, ensuring comprehensive protection and advocacy for our clients. We understand that disputes may arise from contested trust terms, perceived inequity among beneficiaries, or allegations of fiduciary misconduct. Whether the issue involves a trustee allegedly mishandling a trust's assets or conflicts between co-trustees, our priority is to offer strategies that resolve these disputes efficiently.

Many of these disputes arise from how a trust is managed after it has been created. In these situations, working with an attorney familiar with both litigation and Allen trust administration can help clarify responsibilities and determine whether fiduciary duties have been breached. Our approach to tackling these challenges is customized to align with the unique elements of each case and nurture outcomes that satisfy our client's objectives.

Our Commitment to Client Satisfaction

Why do clients choose Crain & Wooley? Our commitment to transparency, honesty, and client-focused service sets us apart. We offer a 100% money-back guarantee if you're not satisfied with our responsiveness, showcasing our dedication to excellence in every interaction.

Our team is well-versed in Texas state laws and proceedings relevant to trust litigation, ensuring your case is handled with the utmost knowledge and care. Our experience in the Allen court systems allows us to navigate local laws effectively, providing peace of mind during complex processes.

Clients choose us not just for our legal acumen but for our steadfast commitment to their satisfaction. We believe in a client-centered approach that emphasizes understanding each individual case's unique challenges and objectives. From the moment you engage our services, we work closely with you to tailor our approach to fit your specific needs. Our legal team remains accessible throughout the litigation process, ready to answer questions and address concerns that may arise.

When you work with our team, you benefit from:

Consistent communication: We return calls and emails promptly and keep you informed about developments, hearings, and negotiation efforts.

Customized legal strategies: We align our recommendations with your goals, risk tolerance, and family dynamics rather than forcing a one-size-fits-all approach.

Support beyond the courtroom: We help you understand how trust litigation may affect your broader estate plan, including related tools such as Allen trusts and other estate planning documents, so you can reduce the risk of future disputes.

Common Trust Disputes We Handle

When you contact us about a trust conflict, you may not be sure whether your situation even qualifies as trust litigation or what options are available. We regularly assist trustees, beneficiaries, and family members with disputes that arise in Collin County courts, including those heard at the county courthouse that serves residents of Allen and surrounding communities. By understanding the types of matters we see most often, you can better evaluate whether it is time to speak with a trust litigation attorney Allen families rely on for clear guidance.

Many cases involve questions about a trustee's conduct, such as failing to provide accountings, delaying distributions, or making investments that appear inconsistent with the terms of the trust. Other disputes center on claims that a trust amendment was signed when the trustor lacked capacity or was unduly influenced by someone who stood to benefit. We also see situations where siblings or other beneficiaries disagree about how important assets should be valued or whether certain expenses should be paid from the trust, and these disagreements can quickly escalate without knowledgeable counsel.

These disputes may also intersect with other estate planning matters. For example, conflicts sometimes arise when trust assets overlap with probate issues handled by an Allen probate attorney or when disagreements about beneficiary rights stem from the way estate planning documents were originally structured.

In many of these situations, we help clients understand and assert their rights through:

Investigating trustee actions: Reviewing accountings, correspondence, and transaction histories to determine whether fiduciary duties have been met.

Challenging or defending trust changes: Evaluating medical records, witness statements, and drafting history when the validity of a trust or amendment is in question.

Addressing conflicts among beneficiaries: Guiding difficult family conversations and, when necessary, presenting issues to the probate court for a binding decision.

Coordinating with related probate matters: Aligning trust litigation strategy with any ongoing probate case to avoid inconsistent results or duplicated effort.

Because every family and every trust is different, we take time to learn how the conflict developed, what relationships you hope to preserve, and what outcome would feel workable for you in the long term. A trust litigation lawyer Allen residents work with should be prepared to weigh both legal and practical considerations, including the emotional toll of extended court proceedings. We help you compare the risks and benefits of negotiation, mediation, and trial so you can choose a path that fits your goals and your family dynamics.

The Trust Litigation Process: What to Expect

Familiarizing yourself with the trust litigation process can help manage uncertainties. Here's a clear view of what you can anticipate when working with us:

Initial Consultation: We start with a comprehensive consultation to assess your situation and tailor our approach to your needs.

Case Evaluation & Strategy: Our attorneys review the trust documents and any concerns to determine the best legal strategy.

Mediation & Negotiation: Whenever possible, we aim to resolve disputes amicably through mediation, saving time and resources.

Litigation: If needed, we are prepared to zealously represent your interests in court, drawing on our extensive litigation experience.

Resolution & Follow-Up: Once the case is resolved, we ensure all outcomes are implemented correctly, respecting your legacy.

The initial consultation is a critical first step where we gather information, understand your objectives, and set realistic expectations. This preliminary meeting establishes our commitment to building a relationship based on trust and transparency. Our detailed case evaluation involves scrutinizing trust documents and any alleged breaches, allowing us to construct a strategy that reflects your personal and legal priorities. Each phase of the litigation process is designed to protect your interests and secure a favorable resolution.

Throughout mediation and negotiation phases, our attorneys leverage their skills to explore solutions that minimize confrontation. We find that many trust disputes are best resolved outside of court, and we focus our efforts on achieving settlements that reconcile differences and maintain familial bonds. However, when court intervention becomes essential, our litigation team is ready to advocate rigorously on your behalf, utilizing their familiarity with local courts and precedents to bolster your case. Our follow-up ensures the complete execution of case outcomes, providing a smooth path toward closure and peace of mind.

Frequently Asked Questions

What Is Trust Litigation?

Trust litigation involves legal proceedings concerning the disputes over how a trust is interpreted or administered. This can include challenges to a trustee's actions, beneficiary disputes, or disagreements about the terms of the trust. At Crain & Wooley, our focus is on resolving these issues efficiently and equitably, ensuring the trustor's wishes are fulfilled while protecting beneficiaries' rights.

How Long Does Trust Litigation Take?

The duration of trust litigation can vary considerably depending on the complexity of the case, the number of parties involved, and the willingness of the parties to negotiate. While some disputes are resolved quickly through mediation, others may require more extensive litigation, which could take months or even years. We are committed to providing timely updates and working towards a resolution that minimizes the impact on your life.

Should I Attempt to Resolve Trust Issues Myself?

While some straightforward issues might be resolved through direct communication, trust disputes often involve complex legal principles that are best handled with professional assistance. Attempting to manage these alone without legal experience can lead to misunderstandings or unfavorable outcomes. Our team at Crain & Wooley is here to offer guidance, ensuring your actions are legally sound and your rights fully protected.

Contact Crain & Wooley Today

Allen Will Attorney

Crafting Wills with Integrity & Transparency

At Crain & Wooley, we understand the importance of a well-drafted will to secure your family's future. Our will lawyers in Allen are dedicated to providing clarity and peace of mind through comprehensive will creation and estate planning. By choosing us, you benefit from our professional approach.

The Importance of Having a Will

Having a will is essential to safeguarding your assets and ensuring they are distributed according to your wishes. Without one, Texas intestacy laws determine how your estate is divided, which may not align with your intentions. A properly drafted will gives you control and can reduce confusion during the Allen probate process.

Drafting a will involves complex legal considerations, including:

Naming Beneficiaries: Clearly identify who will inherit your assets.

Choosing Executors: Appoint individuals who will manage estate distribution.

Guardianship Designations: Name guardians for minor children.

Asset Protection: Implement strategies to preserve wealth for future generations.

Moreover, a well-crafted will allows you to address the unique needs of your family members, including provisions for any dependents with special needs. This ensures that their care is not left to the discretion of others but is handled according to your specified instructions. Our firm can assist in adding specific clauses that reflect your unique family dynamics, providing you with peace of mind that your unique situations are solemnly addressed.

Understanding Will Laws & Regulations in Allen

Allen, as part of Texas, adheres to state-specific regulations that govern will creation. It's vital to ensure your will complies with these laws to be deemed valid and enforceable. At Crain & Wooley, we stay informed about any updates or changes to Texas estate laws, taking the burden off your shoulders and allowing you to focus on what matters most.

Some critical aspects of Texas will laws include:

Witness Requirements: A valid will must generally be signed in the presence of at least two witnesses.

Testamentary Capacity: You must be of sound mind and at least 18 years old, or otherwise legally able to make a will.

Self-Proving Affidavit: Option to attach an affidavit that simplifies probate proceedings.

No Contest Clause: This can deter beneficiaries from challenging the will.

Texas allows for both typed and handwritten wills, provided they meet the legal requirements. Utilizing a self-proving affidavit can expedite the probate process, making it a practical option for many. Our attorneys can guide you through the adept use of these options, protecting your estate from unnecessary delays and costs.

Working With an Allen Will Attorney

Many individuals postpone planning simply because they are unsure what to expect. During an initial meeting, we review your family structure, assets, and long-term goals. We then outline how your will fits within your broader Allen estate planning strategy and explain how related documents work together.

We also discuss practical considerations, such as coordinating beneficiary designations on retirement accounts and life insurance policies. These details are often overlooked in online templates but are critical to ensuring your estate plan functions properly.

Throughout the process, we maintain clear communication so you understand what is being drafted, what decisions remain, and how your documents will operate under Texas law.

FAQ: Common Questions About Wills in Allen, Texas

What Happens If I Die Without a Will in Allen?

In Texas, if you pass away without a will, your estate will be subject to the state's intestacy laws. These laws dictate how assets are distributed, often favoring immediate family members. However, this might not reflect personal wishes or provide for specific friends, charities, or distant relatives. Having a will ensures your assets are distributed as you intend, providing peace of mind and reducing potential family disputes. At Crain & Wooley, we assist in drafting a comprehensive will to reflect your desires.

How Often Should I Update My Will?

It's crucial to regularly update your will to reflect changes in your personal circumstances, assets, or family structure. Key times to review and potentially revise your will include after major life events such as marriage, divorce, the birth of a child, or acquiring significant assets. We recommend reviewing your will every three to five years or when major changes occur. Our Allen-based will lawyers can assist you in making necessary updates, ensuring your estate plan remains current and comprehensive.

Can I Write My Own Will in Texas?

While Texas allows the creation of handwritten (holographic) wills, complexities often arise, potentially leading to disputes or invalidation during probate. A professional will attorney can help ensure your document is legally robust, minimizing the risk of complications. At Crain & Wooley, we provide guidance and legal knowledge, securing your intentions and protecting your family's future through a legally sound will.

What Are Executor Responsibilities in Allen?

Being an executor involves managing the deceased's estate, including settling debts, managing estate assets, and distributing inheritance as outlined in the will. This role is both a responsibility and a fiduciary obligation, requiring careful execution. Selecting a reliable executor is crucial, and understanding these duties can help you choose wisely. Our team assists executors in navigating these responsibilities, ensuring the estate is settled in accordance with the testator's wishes.

For many families in Allen and the surrounding Collin County communities, the the probate process typically moves through local courts that handle estates on a regular basis, and having guidance from a will lawyer who understands how those courts operate can make each step feel more manageable. We can walk you and your chosen executor through what to expect from initial filings to final distributions, helping you prepare documents and timelines that fit your particular situation rather than relying on generic forms. By pairing practical explanations with clear communication, we aim to reduce uncertainty so your executor can focus on honoring your wishes instead of worrying about procedural missteps.

Contact Us Today for Peace of Mind Tomorrow

Creating a will with Crain & Wooley protects your legacy and provides security for your loved ones. Our commitment to integrity, transparency, and personal attention sets us apart in Allen's legal landscape. Understanding your unique circumstances, we provide tailored estate planning solutions that are clear, comprehensive, and cost-effective.

When you meet with our team, we take time to learn about your family, your assets, and your goals so we can recommend a planning approach that fits your stage of life and your comfort level. Whether you need a straightforward will, a package that combines your will with powers of attorney, or coordination with an existing trust, we outline your options in plain language and explain how Texas law will treat each choice. We also discuss how your plan will work in practice if your loved ones ever need to appear in a local probate court, so you can feel confident that what you sign today will provide clear direction tomorrow.

Don't leave your family's future uncertain. Call us at (972) 945-1610 to schedule your consultation today—because planning for tomorrow starts with a call today.

Estate Planning Services in Arlington, TX

Residents in Arlington benefit from comprehensive estate planning services that cater to local legal nuances. Texas law affects everything from probate proceedings to how wills are executed. We assist clients in navigating these complexities with precision and care.
