In the event that you become incapacitated or are otherwise incapable of making healthcare decisions for yourself, a Medical Power of Attorney (“MPOA”) can help resolve the issue by empowering a trusted agent of your choosing to step in and make informed medical decisions on your behalf, ensuring that your healthcare preferences are respected and the best possible choices are made in line with your values and wishes.

At Crain & Wooley, our seasoned DFW attorneys have extensive experience helping Texans prepare for the future. Since 2018, our compassionate firm has committed to delivering sound counsel and high-end representation to families in life's toughest seasons. Our in-depth legal knowledge makes us well-equipped to guide the steps of you and your loved ones wisely and effectively throughout the complexities of Texas probate and estate planning.

Our seasoned lawyers offer consultations to help Texans plan for the future. Call (972) 945-1610 to request an appointment with a skilled attorney in DFW.

What Is a Medical Power of Attorney (MPOA)?

In Texas, a durable Power of Attorney or POA is a legal document that allows Texans to designate a trusted party to make important decisions—such as financial, medical, and other legal decisions—on their behalf in the event that they become incapacitated, or are otherwise incapable of making these decisions for themselves. POAs can take various forms, including financial POAs.

A Medical Power of Attorney is more than just a piece of paper—it's a legally binding document to safeguard your future health and wellbeing. An MPOA allows you to appoint a trusted individual or “agent” to make healthcare decisions on your behalf if you become incapacitated.

Selecting an Agent for a Texas MPOA

Creating a legally effective (“durable”) MPOA can ensure that a person’s healthcare wishes are respected and carried out, even if they can't communicate them independently due to incapacitation or illness. The appointed agent in MPOAs is granted the authority to make a wide range of health-related decisions, from consenting to or refusing medical treatment to deciding long-term care options.

However, the power of the appointed MPOA agent is limited. The agent is legally obligated to act in the principal's best interests while complying with all terms and conditions outlined in the document.

Legal Requirements for MPOAs in Texas

To create a valid MPOA, certain legal requirements must be met. Texas law requires the principal to read a disclosure statement explaining the implications of an MPOA before signing to express their agreement and understanding of its terms and conditions. For an MPOA to be legally valid, the principal must sign the MPOA before two witnesses or have their signature acknowledged by a notary public.

When choosing an agent, it’s essential to consider their willingness and ability to make potentially difficult decisions for your life and health. The principal may also benefit from discussing their healthcare preferences with potential agents in advance to gain a better understanding of that person’s approach and capabilities.

Can a Texas MPOA Be Revoked or Modified?

Yes. You can revoke or modify your MPOA at any point, as long as you demonstrate the mental competence to do so.

Living Wills vs. MPOAs: What’s the Difference?

Both MPOAs and living wills are essential components of Texas estate planning, but they aren’t the same. Each serves its own distinct purpose when it comes to Texas law.

A living will is a legal document that outlines your end-of-life healthcare preferences. Unlike the MPOA, living wills do not appoint an agent to make decisions for you. Instead, it declares your wishes regarding life-sustaining treatments and medical care in the event of a terminal condition or permanent unconsciousness.

/estate-planning/pre-postnuptial-agreeme.body:
Dallas-Fort Worth Prenuptial & Postnuptial Agreements Attorneys

Maintain Control of the Assets You Own

At Crain & Wooley, we understand that getting married is one of the most important decisions that you will make in your life, which is why our team of compassionate and knowledgeable legal professionals is here to help couples protect the hard-earned assets they are bringing into the marriage.

If your goal is to avoid major disputes with your future or current spouse, then please get in touch with our prenuptial and postnuptial agreements attorneys in DFW to discuss how your marital assets and debts should be distributed if the marriage is dissolved in the future.

Contact us today by calling (972) 945-1610 to learn more about how we can help secure stability for your future.

What Does a Prenuptial or Postnuptial Agreement Do?

A nuptial agreement is a legally binding document that determines what happens to your assets in the event your marriage ends in divorce. The purpose of prenuptial and postnuptial agreements is to establish what assets will never become marital property.

These types of agreements cover all of the items that would be addressed if the spouses were to separate or divorce and also override statutory estate rights and statutory requirements for beneficiaries on various accounts that a spouse would otherwise be entitled to.

The following issues can be addressed with a properly drafted prenuptial agreement:

Which spouse will be responsible for paying alimony or spousal support
Which spouse has the right to sell or lease real estate property acquired before or during the marriage
Whether the spouses will be included or eliminated from a will
How life insurance proceeds will be distributed
Establishing care and support for children from a previous marriage
Determining whether pre-marriage debt should be portioned or divided

Prenup vs Postnup: What is the Difference?

Prenuptial and postnuptial agreements are essentially the same thing except for when they are signed. A prenup is signed prior to the marriage and a postnup is signed after the marriage.

The main difference between a prenup and a postnup is that a prenuptial agreement will become valid upon marriage, however, a postnuptial agreement will need to be reviewed by the court before it can be enforceable.

What Can a Prenup NOT Do?

Pre and postnuptial agreements only determine what happens to your financial holdings, property, and other assets in the event of divorce. They do not determine other factors in a divorce, such as child custody or child support. You also cannot use a prenup to enforce restrictions on your spouse, such as maintaining a certain height, weight, hair color, etc.

Call a Dallas-Fort Worth prenup attorney from Crain & Wooley at (972) 945-1610 to get help drafting your pre or postnuptial agreement today.

What Are the Benefits of Prenuptial & Postnuptial Agreements?

If you have significant assets or are expecting a large inheritance, divorce could spell financial disaster for you and any heirs you have or may have. Having a prenup or postnup can offer you and your family valuable protection.

Spouses who choose to use prenuptial and postnuptial agreements gain the following benefits:

Couples must discuss sensitive financial matters, which can limit future misunderstandings
Ease concerns if one spouse is worried the other is seeking to marry for financial gain
Can save money and time on divorce matters
Postnups can be used to address major life events, such as the death of a family member or an inheritance received by one of the partners

Let Us Draft Your Legal Documents Today

Drafting a premarital or postnuptial agreement without guidance from an experienced lawyer can be complicated. In order for the agreement to be valid, it must be in writing, signed by both parties, and include a formal Statement of Assets. Whether you need help drafting an enforceable prenuptial agreement or challenging the execution of a postnuptial agreement, you can count on our legal team to stand in your corner and advocate for your best interests.

If you would like to set up a case consultation with Crain & Wooley, please use our convenient online scheduler or give us a call at (972) 945-1610 today.

Commonly Asked Questions

What is the difference between a prenup vs postnup?

A prenuptial agreement is a contract that a couple signs and agrees to before marriage. This outlines all the terms of divorce in case that were to occur. A postnuptial agreement, in simple terms, is also a prenup; it is just created after the couple is already married.

Why do people use prenuptial & postnuptial agreements?

The purpose of prenuptial and postnuptial agreements is to establish what assets will never become marital property. These types of agreements cover all of the items that would be addressed if the spouses were to separate or divorce and also override statutory estate rights and statutory requirements for beneficiaries on various accounts that a spouse would otherwise be entitled to.

What are the benefits of prenuptial & postnuptial agreements?

Spouses who choose to use prenuptial and postnuptial agreements gain the following benefits: Couples must discuss sensitive financial matters, which can limit future misunderstandings; Ease concerns if one spouse is worried the other is seeking to marry for financial gain; Can save money and time on divorce matters; Postnups can be used to address major life events, such as the death of a family member or an inheritance received by one of the partners.

h2s: Understanding Living Trusts in Texas: A Primer
What to Include in Your Texas Revocable Living Trust
Who Should Create a Revocable Living Trust in Texas?
FAQ: Revocable Living Trusts

/estate-planning/revocable-living-trusts.body:
A revocable living trust is a trust arrangement that allows a person to transfer assets into a trust while still alive. The trust is then managed by a trustee, which can be an individual or a financial institution. The trustee then distributes the assets according to the terms of the trust agreement. This type of trust is very useful for people who are elderly, disabled, or have minor children, as it allows them to avoid probate and the costs associated with it.

It is also beneficial for people who want to avoid the public nature of probate and the potential for creditors to claim their assets. A trust can be created in any manner that the person wishes, and it can be changed or revoked at any time.

Understanding Living Trusts in Texas: A Primer

A living trust in Texas is a legal document that allows individuals (known as the grantor or settlor) to establish a trust relationship with a trustee and transfer ownership of their assets into the trust while they are alive. The grantor retains control over the assets during their lifetime and can make changes or revoke the trust, if desired. Upon the grantor's passing, the assets held in the living trust can be distributed to the named beneficiaries without the need for probate, providing privacy and potential cost savings.

What to Include in Your Texas Revocable Living Trust

The assets that can be included in a revocable living trust in Texas may vary depending on individual circumstances and preferences. Generally, a wide range of assets can be included, such as: 1. Real estate properties (residential or commercial). 2. Bank accounts (checking, savings, money market). 3. Investment accounts (stocks, bonds, mutual funds, brokerage accounts). 4. Business interests (ownership shares or partnerships). 5. Intellectual property rights (copyrights, patents, trademarks). 6. Personal belongings (artwork, jewelry, collectibles, vehicles). 7. Life insurance policies (the policy itself or the proceeds). 8. Retirement accounts (IRAs or 401(k) plans).

Consulting with an attorney who specializes in estate planning can provide personalized guidance on which assets are suitable for inclusion in a revocable living trust in Texas.

Who Should Create a Revocable Living Trust in Texas?

A revocable living trust is a useful estate planning tool that can be beneficial to many people. However, it is particularly useful for people who are elderly, disabled, or have minor children. In each of these cases, it allows the person to avoid probate and the costs associated with it.

Having a revocable living trust is also beneficial if an individual owns properties in more than one state. Having a trust established eliminates the tedious, time-consuming, and expensive multi-state probate process.

FAQ: Revocable Living Trusts

What is a revocable living trust? A revocable living trust is a legal arrangement that allows you to transfer assets into a trust while you're still alive. You retain control over the trust and can make changes or revoke it as needed. Upon your passing, the trust assets are distributed according to your instructions, avoiding probate and associated costs.

What are the benefits of a revocable living trust? Avoidance of Probate; Privacy; Flexibility (you can alter or revoke the trust at any time while you're alive); Asset Protection (helps prevent creditors from claiming assets).

What assets can be included in a revocable living trust? Real Estate; Bank Accounts; Investment Accounts; Business Interests; Intellectual Property; Personal Belongings; Life Insurance Policies; Retirement Accounts.

Who should consider creating a revocable living trust? Elderly or Disabled Individuals; Parents with Minor Children; Owners of Out-of-State Properties; Those Seeking Privacy.

When should I create a revocable living trust? Ideally, a revocable living trust should be established as soon as you have assets you wish to pass on to your beneficiaries after your death. This includes real estate, investments, bank accounts, and other valuable assets.

Strategic Timing for Creating Your Texas Trust

A trust should be created whenever a person owns property that they wish to pass on to their beneficiaries after they have passed away. This includes homes, vehicles, investments, bank accounts, retirement accounts, and other assets. It is important to create this trust before a person dies, as it allows them to direct where their assets should go after they have passed away.

It is also beneficial for people who want to avoid the public nature of probate and the potential for creditors to claim their assets.

/estate-planning/supplemental-needs-trus.body:
At Crain & Wooley, we understand that caring for a loved one with special needs requires both heart and planning. Families in the Dallas-Fort Worth area face unique challenges when balancing daily responsibilities with long-term financial and legal considerations. Our attorneys combine compassion and practical experience to help you create a comprehensive plan that ensures your loved one is protected—today and in the future.

Every family's journey is unique, and so are their needs. We approach each situation with care, taking time to consider the daily realities your loved ones face. In the Dallas-Fort Worth metroplex, local programs and agencies offer services that can become an important part of a long-term plan.

In addition to creating customized plans, our firm is committed to offering ongoing support and advice as your family's circumstances evolve. We regularly review and update your plan to accommodate any changes in legislation or personal circumstances.

For families concerned about preserving eligibility for government benefits, we provide guidance on integrating assets so these benefits stay protected. The laws regarding special needs trusts can be complex, but we navigate these details so you don't have to.

Understanding Special Needs Planning

Special needs planning means developing a strategy to provide for the financial and legal needs of a person with a disability. This comprehensive approach includes not just creating a special needs trust but also assigning guardianship, setting up advance directives, and understanding the role of pooled trusts. It also involves financial planning such as insurance and savings strategies to cover unexpected expenses or changes in care needs.

We consider it vital to educate family members and caregivers about their roles and responsibilities. This process includes creating a special needs trust, durable power of attorney, and other legal documents to ensure the person is cared for and their financial future stays secure.

Special needs planning enhances quality of life by creating a framework for family and caregivers to manage daily tasks, prepare for emergencies, and access necessary services.

Types of Special Needs Trusts & Funding Options in Texas

Families in Texas have several ways to protect loved ones with disabilities through specialized trusts. First-party trusts use assets that belong to the person with disabilities, often from an inheritance or personal injury award. Third-party trusts use funds from relatives or friends who want to provide for the person without disrupting eligibility for government programs. Some families benefit from pooled trusts, which nonprofit organizations manage for a group of beneficiaries.

Choosing the right trust option in Fort Worth depends on your goals and funding sources. Each has its own advantages and requirements under Texas law. A well-crafted trust protects eligibility for vital programs such as Medicaid while also controlling how assets are used to meet personal needs.

Key Steps to Creating a Special Needs Trust in Fort Worth

Setting up a special needs trust in Fort Worth involves a series of important steps. After selecting a trust type suited to your loved one's needs, you work with an attorney focused on special needs planning to outline the terms. Clear agreements about the trustee, beneficiary rights, and designated uses for funds keep the trust effective and compliant with Texas and federal law. The trust requires funding, either upfront or over time, through gifts, inheritances, or structured settlements.

During this process, families can expect court communication and oversight, especially if the trust receives significant funding. Tarrant County courts have clear procedures for approving and tracking these trusts.

Common Pitfalls & How to Avoid Them in Texas Special Needs Planning

Families in the Fort Worth area may run into pitfalls when planning special needs trusts, particularly as state and federal rules change. One common challenge is incorrect fund distribution, which can cause the beneficiary to lose crucial government benefits. Another risk is choosing a trustee who lacks time or relevant skills to manage reporting and legal tasks.

You can avoid these issues by carefully selecting the trustee and conducting periodic reviews to adjust for changes in personal or legal situations. Ongoing support lets you proactively adapt your plan.

How Does the Local Process Affect Timing & Outcomes?

In Fort Worth, the timeline for special needs trust approval and funding may depend on specific Tarrant County court processes. Local courts review and approve trust documents, especially in cases involving public benefits or large assets. Preparing documents thoroughly from the start minimizes delays. Local agencies and support networks, such as the Tarrant County Department of Human Services and Fort Worth ISD's transition services, frequently interact with families during planning or review phases.

How Our Flat-Rate & Lifetime Update Options Help Fort Worth Families

Flat-rate pricing brings families peace of mind by providing clear expectations for cost and service scope. Fort Worth families get upfront clarity, knowing what's included and how much the service costs—no matter how many questions or revisions.

Our lifetime guarantee option goes further. As Texas law or your personal situation changes, you can choose ongoing document reviews and updates. This proactive approach keeps your trust and related documents effective over time.

Why Special Needs Planning Is Important

Special needs planning is important because it allows you to provide for your loved ones as they grow. It also allows you to protect your loved one from becoming a public charge. If your loved one receives public benefits, they may lose those benefits if they receive gifts or inheritances. Special needs planning allows you to provide for your loved one while protecting their benefits.

Special needs planning can help you coordinate care across multiple providers and agencies in the Fort Worth area. It is also important to plan for the future because your loved one's needs will change over time. Additionally, planning ahead protects against sudden changes in your financial situation or health. Special needs planning is not just about securing resources, but also about empowering individuals with disabilities to lead fulfilling lives.

Benefits of Special Needs Planning

Special needs planning provides numerous benefits for you and your loved one. The benefits include: Providing for your loved one; Protecting your loved one from becoming a public charge; Helping you adjust as your loved one's needs change.

Families in Fort Worth who plan ahead gain a clearer picture of available supports, such as Medicaid waivers and state-based benefits available in Texas. Additional benefits include better communication and understanding among family members. Thoughtful special needs planning reduces financial strain by combining public insurance programs, tax benefits, and other resources.

How Our Supplemental Needs Trust Lawyer in Fort Worth Can Help You

Planning for the future of a loved one with special needs involves complex legal and financial decisions. Maintaining eligibility for vital government benefits while securing financial stability requires experience and consideration.

At Crain & Wooley, our Dallas-Fort Worth special needs trust attorney can help you by: Personalized trust planning - we take time to learn your family's specific circumstances, concerns, and goals before tailoring a supplemental needs trust. Protecting government benefit eligibility - we know the rules that govern these benefits and structure your trust to ensure your loved one keeps receiving support. Comprehensive asset management - from choosing the right trustee to supporting ongoing trust administration. Advocacy and legal representation - we advocate for your family's interests in all legal matters tied to the trust. Future planning and peace of mind - we develop a long-term plan that anticipates life changes.

We discuss opportunities and potential hurdles arising from Texas laws and Tarrant County processes. Additionally, education is core to our services; we offer workshops and resources to help families understand the details and options in special needs planning.

The Legal Landscape for Special Needs Trusts in Fort Worth

Understanding the legal landscape for special needs trusts in Fort Worth is crucial for effective planning. Texas law allows for the establishment of several types of trusts suited to different situations, including first-party and third-party special needs trusts. Each type has its own benefits and limitations.

Texas courts, including those in Tarrant County, regularly handle special needs trust matters and have procedures designed to streamline approval and administration. The process of creating a special needs trust means navigating both state and federal laws, which determine how these trusts interact with Medicaid and Supplemental Security Income (SSI) benefits.

Local Resources and Support for Special Needs Families

Families in the Dallas-Fort Worth area can use a wide variety of local resources designed to assist those with special needs. Collaboration with networks like Fort Worth ISD, the Arc of DFW, and state-run programs can strengthen your overall care plan. These organizations offer education, financial counseling, and guidance that add real value when combined with legal planning.

Fort Worth offers supportive environments through community centers, educational institutions, and healthcare providers. Working with Crain & Wooley connects you with these key resources and brings together legal and local support.

Frequently Asked Questions

What Is a Special Needs Trust? A special needs trust is a legal arrangement that lets a person with a physical or mental disability, or a chronic illness, receive income without threatening eligibility for public disability benefits like Social Security, Supplemental Security Income, Medicare, or Medicaid. A trustee manages the trust, overseeing how funds are distributed according to strict legal requirements.

Who Should Serve as a Trustee? The right trustee will grasp your beneficiary's needs and your family's goals, have solid financial management skills, and understand legal standards. A trustee may be a family member, close friend, or a professional organization such as a bank or trust company. Many families opt for professional trustees to help manage the complexities.

What Happens If My Loved One Needs to Move Out of State? Laws about special needs trusts and eligibility for public benefits differ by state, which can affect trust management. Review state rules and be ready to adjust trust provisions if needed. At Crain & Wooley, we guide families through these transitions.

What Steps Are Involved in Setting Up a Special Needs Trust? Establishing a special needs trust involves several steps, starting with a consultation to determine which trust type suits your family. After drafting the legal documents, you must fund it with assets such as property, inheritances, or other resources. Compliance with federal and state laws is critical throughout this process.

Can Public Benefits Be Affected by a Special Needs Trust? Improperly distributing assets can threaten eligibility for government benefits. When created and managed properly, a special needs trust will not jeopardize SSI and Medicaid eligibility. Funds should pay for non-support expenses like education, medical care, home modifications, or equipment to protect benefit eligibility.

Dallas-Fort Worth Tax Planning Attorneys

Assisting Clients With Tax Planning in Texas

At Crain & Wooley, we help clients of all backgrounds plan for costly estate taxes on the assets they plan to pass on to loved ones and other beneficiaries. Our dedicated legal team in Dallas-Fort Worth is familiar with the various components of gift taxes, estate taxes, and generation-skipping transfer taxes, and we have the extensive resources you need to minimize or avoid tax liability.

Call Crain & Wooley today at (972) 945-1610 or contact us online to schedule a consultation with our tax planning attorney in Dallas-Fort Worth.

What is Tax Planning?

Tax planning is considered the organization of finances to minimize tax liability while complying with tax laws and regulations. It often involves analyzing your financial situation, identifying possible tax-saving opportunities, and implementing strategies to optimize your tax position. Effective tax planning helps individuals and businesses maximize their after-tax income, preserve wealth, and achieve financial goals.

Tax Planning Matters We Handle

Call or visit our law office today to discuss any of the following tax planning strategies:

Charitable Giving: You can take income tax deductions for money or assets you donate to charity. You can also use charitable remainder trusts, charitable lead trusts, or family foundations to avoid capital gains and estate taxes.
Assets in Trust: There are different types of trusts that can be used to protect assets and reduce the size of your taxable estate. We can help you determine if your estate plan should include grantor retained annuity trusts (GRATs), irrevocable life insurance trusts (ILITs), education trusts, or special needs trusts.
Annual Gifting: You can transfer money or assets to your family members and other loved ones to reduce the total value of your estate. This tax strategy lowers the amount of estate taxes that will be due after you pass away. Annual gifting allows your assets to be transferred to beneficiaries without incurring costly gift and estate taxes.

How Our Tax Planning Attorney Can Help You

We can help you by:

Strategic Tax Planning: We work closely with individuals, families, and businesses to develop customized tax strategies aligned with their short-term and long-term financial objectives. Whether you're looking to minimize income taxes, reduce estate taxes, or optimize business tax efficiency, we can help you navigate the complexities of the tax code and identify opportunities to legally reduce your tax burden.
Income Tax Optimization: With our in-depth knowledge of federal, state, and local tax laws, we can assist you in structuring your income and investments tax-efficiently. From maximizing deductions and credits to exploring tax-deferred investment options, we'll help you make informed decisions to minimize your current and future tax liabilities.
Estate and Gift Tax Planning: Estate and gift taxes can significantly erode the wealth you've worked hard to build over a lifetime. We can help you develop comprehensive estate plans that minimize estate taxes and ensure your assets are transferred to your heirs according to your wishes. Whether you need assistance with wills, trusts, or other estate planning tools, we'll provide personalized guidance to protect your legacy and preserve wealth for future generations.
Business Tax Strategies: As business owners, we understand the importance of sufficient tax planning for small businesses, startups, and corporations. We can assist you in navigating the very complex landscape of business taxation, including entity selection, restructuring, mergers and acquisitions, and succession planning. By optimizing your business's tax structure and leveraging available tax incentives, we'll help you maximize profitability and minimize tax exposure.
IRS Representation and Tax Compliance: Dealing with the IRS can be daunting, especially if facing an audit or tax dispute. We can provide skilled representation in IRS proceedings and negotiations, helping you resolve tax issues efficiently and effectively. We'll ensure you comply with tax laws and regulations while protecting your rights and interests.
Year-Round Tax Guidance: Tax planning is not a once-a-year task—it's an ongoing process that requires careful attention and proactive strategies. We are available year-round to provide guidance, answer questions, and assist with tax-related decisions as your financial circumstances evolve. Whether you're planning a major life event, such as marriage or retirement, or navigating changes in tax laws, we'll be by your side every step of the way.

Contact Our Tax Planning Attorneys in Dallas-Fort Worth Today

Our talented and knowledgeable legal team is committed to serving clients of all backgrounds who need advice with complicated estate planning and tax matters. We take great pride in using our extensive resources to customize unique and effective tax planning strategies that are suited to our clients' various needs.

Call (972) 945-1610 or contact us online today to get started with our Dallas-Fort Worth tax planning lawyers!

/estate-planning/trusts.body:
Trusts aren't only for high-net-worth estates. Families with real estate, minor children, blended households, or a loved one with special needs commonly benefit from trust-based planning. At Crain & Wooley, we serve Fort Worth clients with the same focused estate planning approach we bring to every client across the DFW area: flat-rate pricing with no hidden fees, an optional lifetime document review, and a 100% money-back service commitment if we don't respond to your important matters within our promised timeframe.

We hold memberships in NAELA (National Academy of Elder Law Attorneys), ElderCounsel, and the Texas Bar College. Our work stays focused on estate planning and related fields. We don't spread across unrelated practice areas. In-person appointments are available throughout DFW, including by appointment in Fort Worth, and virtual consultations are available for clients throughout Tarrant County and beyond.

What Is a Trust?

A trust is a legal arrangement in which a grantor transfers ownership of assets to a trustee, who manages those assets for the benefit of one or more beneficiaries. Unlike a will, a properly funded trust doesn't require a probate court proceeding to transfer assets after the grantor's death. That distinction matters: assets held in a revocable living trust pass outside of Tarrant County Probate Court, preserving privacy and reducing administrative delay for your family.

Trusts also give you control a will simply can't match. You can set conditions on timing and method of distribution, restrict access for beneficiaries who may not be ready to manage an inheritance, and address contingencies a straightforward will would leave to a judge's discretion. A pour-over will can work alongside your trust to capture any assets not transferred into the trust during your lifetime, directing them into the trust at death so nothing falls through the cracks.

Types of Trusts We Establish & Administer

There's no single trust that fits every family. We work with you to identify which structure fits your goals, then draft, fund, and administer it correctly.

We establish and administer the following types of trusts: Special Needs Trust; Revocable Living Trust; Irrevocable Trust; Minor Trusts; Descendant's Trusts; Educational Trust; Taxable Estate Trusts; Spendthrift Trusts; Grantor Retained Annuity Trusts (GRATs); Charitable Trusts.

Spendthrift trusts protect beneficiaries from their own potentially harmful financial habits by restricting direct access to trust principal. GRATs are designed to minimize gift and estate taxes when transferring appreciating assets to heirs. Charitable trusts allow grantors to support causes while potentially reducing estate tax liability.

We also provide guidance with Trust Administration and Trust Litigation when disputes arise or documents require judicial interpretation.

Why Fort Worth Families Include a Trust in Their Estate Plan

A trust does more than avoid probate. Depending on your family's circumstances, it can protect assets from long-term care costs, shield a beneficiary's public benefits eligibility, and address complications that Texas community property law creates for blended families.

Common reasons Fort Worth and Tarrant County families establish trusts: Avoiding In-State and Out-of-State Probate - a funded trust passes assets outside of probate; if you own real estate in multiple states, a trust can eliminate the need to open ancillary probate proceedings in each state. Planning for Nursing Home Expenses - an irrevocable trust can protect assets from being consumed by long-term care costs; the greatest benefit comes from having a nursing home plan in place at least five years before the anticipated need, due to the Medicaid look-back period. Protecting Loved Ones with Special Needs - a special needs trust preserves a beneficiary's eligibility for means-tested public benefits such as Medicaid and SSI. Protecting Assets from Troubled Heirs - a revocable living trust can restrict how and when distributions reach beneficiaries. Protecting a Surviving Spouse - Texas is a community property state, and in blended family situations, a deceased spouse's half of community property can pass to children from a prior marriage rather than to the surviving spouse; a trust can provide a court-free path. Providing for Minor Children or Grandchildren - trusts can hold and manage a minor's inheritance until a suitable age or milestone is reached. Addressing Blended Family Concerns - a trust gives you precise control over how assets are distributed among all family members. Avoiding Estate Taxes - a trust may reduce the tax burden on your estate and beneficiaries. Supporting Charities - a charitable trust lets you leave a legacy of generosity with specific instructions.

Why Fort Worth Clients Choose Crain & Wooley

For clients evaluating a trust attorney in Fort Worth families can rely on, pricing transparency and responsiveness are among the most common concerns. We address both directly.

Flat-Rate Pricing: Proactive trust planning services are billed at a clear, upfront flat-rate pricing with no hourly billing surprises for standard planning work.

Optional Lifetime Service Commitment: Life changes. So does Texas law. Our optional lifetime service commitment provides automatic document review and updates over time so your plan stays current and effective.

Money-Back Responsiveness Service Commitment: We promise you won't be left waiting. If you don't hear back from us on important matters within our committed timeframe, we offer a 100% money-back service commitment under specified conditions.

NAELA & ElderCounsel Membership: Our NAELA membership reflects our background in elder law and special needs planning. ElderCounsel membership provides access to advanced drafting tools. Texas Bar College membership recognizes our commitment to ongoing legal education.

Client Education: We host free seminars and publish guides and videos so you understand every piece of your plan before you sign anything.

Flexible Access: Both in-person appointments in the DFW area and virtual consultations are available. Fort Worth clients can meet with us by appointment, and we serve families throughout Tarrant County and the broader metro.

The Role of a Wills and Trusts Attorney in Fort Worth

A trust doesn't work in isolation. It functions best when coordinated with a pour-over will, durable power of attorney, and healthcare directives. Each document fills a distinct role without creating gaps or conflicts. Our attorneys draft and coordinate all of these instruments together.

We're familiar with Tarrant County Probate Court No. 1 and Probate Court No. 2, both operating from the Tarrant County Courthouse in Fort Worth.

Asset Titling for Texas Real Estate: Real estate must be deeded into the trust to function as intended. Texas homestead law requires specific deed language to preserve homestead protections and tax exemptions. Mineral Rights Planning: For Fort Worth families with oil and mineral rights, a revocable living trust provides a straightforward path for generational transfer without probate court involvement. Multi-State Property: If you own property in other states, a properly funded trust can eliminate the need for ancillary probate proceedings in each state. Asset Protection Strategy: We provide strategies tailored to Tarrant County families, including blended family situations, special needs planning, and long-term care concerns.

Working with a trust lawyer in Fort Worth means your documents reflect Texas law, local court realities, and the specifics of your family's situation.

We Can Help You Choose the Right Trust Today

With all the options you have when it comes to creating a trust, it's in your best interest to speak with an estate planning attorney at Crain & Wooley. We can walk you through your choices, coordinate all of the necessary documents, and design a trust-based plan that reflects your goals and your family.

Frequently Asked Questions About Trusts

Can a Trust Be Changed? Revocable trusts can be modified or revoked by the grantor at any time during their lifetime. Irrevocable trusts generally can't be changed after creation, though Texas law provides limited pathways such as a non-judicial settlement agreement with beneficiary consent or a court order.

Can a Trustee Remove a Beneficiary From a Trust in Texas? In most cases, no. The power to add or remove beneficiaries belongs to the grantor, not the trustee. After the grantor's death, when the trust becomes irrevocable, changes to beneficiaries typically require a court petition.

Can a Trustee Also Be a Beneficiary of an Irrevocable Trust in Texas? Yes. Texas law permits a trustee to also serve as a beneficiary, and this arrangement is common in family trusts. Because the trustee still owes a fiduciary duty to all beneficiaries, careful drafting is required.

How Long Does It Take to Establish a Trust? Simpler trusts can typically be completed within a few weeks. More complex arrangements may take longer. The process includes drafting the trust agreement, signing with proper formalities, and funding the trust by retitling assets.

What Happens if I Move Out of Fort Worth or Texas After Establishing a Trust? Trusts generally remain valid across state lines, but your plan should be reviewed whenever you relocate. Laws governing trust administration, asset titling, and property rights vary by state.

What Is the Process for Changing a Trustee in Texas? For a revocable trust, the grantor can typically appoint a new trustee directly under the trust's terms, without court involvement. For irrevocable trusts, replacing a trustee is more complex and may require consent from all beneficiaries, a non-judicial settlement agreement, or court approval.

This means maintaining clear records, communicating regularly with beneficiaries, and staying informed about changes in relevant laws and regulations. By taking this meticulous approach, trustees and administrators can minimize the risk of errors and ensure the trust operates smoothly and transparently.

The Importance of Professional Trust Administration

Having a trusts administration attorney in Dallas-Fort Worth handle your trust ensures adherence to legal obligations and alleviates the burden on trustees. FAQs About Trust Administration in Dallas-Fort Worth

What Is Trust Administration? Trust administration is the process of managing and distributing the assets within a trust according to the terms set by the trustor. This includes paying debts and taxes, distributing assets to beneficiaries, and maintaining detailed records throughout the process.

Why Do I Need a Trust Administration Attorney in Dallas-Fort Worth? Working with a trusts administration attorney provides valuable legal oversight to navigate complex legal obligations. Texas trusts come with specific requirements that, if not followed, could lead to delays or legal challenges. In addition to offering legal oversight, a trust administration attorney can provide guidance in conflict resolution.

How Does Texas Law Affect Trust Administration? Texas law imposes distinct legal obligations for trust administration, including specific timelines and responsibilities for trustees. Legal requirements can vary significantly by state, which makes expert knowledge crucial for compliance.

Can Trustees Be Held Liable? Yes, trustees can be held personally liable for breaches of fiduciary duty, such as mismanagement of trust assets or failure to comply with the trust's terms. This underscores the importance of having qualified legal assistance to guide trustees through their obligations.

What Are Common Challenges in Trust Administration? Some common challenges include complex asset distributions, beneficiary disputes, and navigating tax implications. In addition, trustees must remain vigilant against fraud and financial mismanagement, which can compromise the integrity of a trust.

/estate-planning/trusts/trust-litigation.body:
At Crain & Wooley, we represent clients in trust litigation matters throughout Dallas-Fort Worth, including disputes before the Dallas County Probate Courts. Partners Justin Crain and Jacob Wooley lead the firm with a commitment to transparency, integrity, and compassion. Because our primary focus is estate planning and related fields, we bring drafting knowledge and trust administration context to every dispute we handle. This is a meaningful advantage when contested trust language is at the center of a case.

We serve clients throughout Dallas and the broader DFW area from offices in Plano and Mansfield, with virtual consultations available for those who prefer remote access. Trust litigation is billed at a reasonable hourly rate, consistent with our transparent service model that distinguishes proactive planning (flat-rate) from reactive litigation work.

Common Trust Disputes We Handle for Dallas-Area Clients

Trust litigation arises when trustees or beneficiaries disagree over the administration, terms, or validity of a trust. Under the Texas Trust Code, trustees owe fiduciary duties including loyalty, care, impartiality, and good faith, along with a duty to furnish an accounting to beneficiaries upon written request. When those duties break down, legal intervention is often necessary.

Breach of Fiduciary Duty: When a trustee fails to act in beneficiaries' best interests, we investigate thoroughly and pursue remedies to protect our clients' rights. Trust Contests: A trust contest challenges the validity of a trust on grounds such as lack of mental capacity, undue influence, fraud, or improper execution. Trustee Removal: Trustees may be removed when they engage in self-dealing, material violations of trust terms, or mismanagement. Mismanagement of Assets: Our team scrutinizes financial records to identify and address discrepancies, including situations involving elder financial abuse or exploitation within a trust context. Disputes Over Amendments & Distributions: When beneficiaries disagree with changes to trust documents or asset allocation, we work to clarify intent and pursue equitable resolutions.

We also provide free seminars and educational resources to help clients understand trust management and the dispute process before and during litigation.

Why Dallas Families Choose Crain & Wooley

Justin Crain is recognized by the Texas Bar College as one of the best-trained attorneys in Texas. The firm holds memberships in NAELA (National Academy of Elder Law Attorneys) and ElderCounsel. Justin also authored Leaving a Legacy: What Every Texan Should Know About Estate Planning, a resource that reflects our belief that informed clients make better decisions at every stage of a dispute.

We've helped hundreds of families in the Dallas-Fort Worth area navigate complex trust and estate matters. Our 100% money-back guarantee applies if you don't hear back from us on important matters. Virtual services have been available since 2018, covering the full process from consultation through resolution, so geography is never a barrier to working with us.

Dallas County Probate Courts & Trust Litigation Venue

Dallas County has three statutory probate courts, each headed by an elected judge. Cases are randomly assigned among the three courts, and each court operates under local rules governing notice, motions, continuances, and case transfers that differ from procedures in other Texas counties. These courts also hold concurrent jurisdiction with district courts to hear trust cases and carry a transfer power that can pull related cases from other counties into the same proceeding.

Our experience with probate courts in both Dallas and Tarrant counties allows us to build strategies tailored to each venue's procedures and expectations. The Dallas-Fort Worth area, encompassing Collin, Dallas, and Tarrant counties, is home to a wide range of trust structures and multigenerational estates involving real estate holdings, business interests, and diverse family structures.

When dealing with complex trust disputes, clients can benefit by considering these key factors: Understanding State Law - Texas trust and probate law governs the administration and distribution of assets. Venue Selection and Local Practice - each county court operates under its own procedures and expectations, which can affect strategy and timing. Family and Financial Considerations - the scope of a trust dispute may include not just financial considerations, but also family relationships that require thoughtful navigation.

These disputes often intersect with broader estate issues. In some cases, solutions may involve reviewing planning safeguards such as long-term care planning or understanding how incapacity can lead to the need for adult guardianship.

Frequently Asked Questions

What Is Trust Litigation? Trust litigation is a legal dispute involving the administration, terms, or validity of a trust. Disputes often arise when trustees and beneficiaries disagree over distributions, investments, or management decisions. Cases may involve breach of fiduciary duty, trust contests, or claims of undue influence.

What Fiduciary Duties Does a Trustee Owe Under Texas Law? Under the Texas Trust Code, a trustee owes duties of loyalty, care, impartiality, and good faith. Trustees must also furnish an accounting to beneficiaries upon written request and avoid self-dealing. Failure to meet these duties can result in personal liability, removal, and court-ordered remedies.

Can a Beneficiary Sue a Trustee in Dallas? Yes. Beneficiaries in Texas may file suit in Dallas County Probate Court to remove a trustee, recover losses, or compel an accounting if the trustee has mismanaged assets, withheld information, or violated trust terms.

Can Trust Disputes Be Kept Confidential? Court proceedings are generally public, but trust disputes can often be kept private through alternative dispute resolution methods such as mediation or arbitration. These processes can avoid public court filings and can be especially useful when sensitive family relationships or significant business interests are involved.

Schedule a Consultation With Crain & Wooley

If you're facing a trust dispute in the Dallas area, don't wait to get answers. We offer both in-person consultations from our offices in Plano and Mansfield and virtual appointments for clients throughout Dallas County and the broader DFW metro. Our focus on estate planning and related fields means we understand trust documents from the inside out, which directly shapes how we approach litigation strategy.

/estate-planning/wills.body:
Crain & Wooley knows that you have worked hard your whole life to provide for your family. That is why we are dedicated to providing clients with the comprehensive legal services and reliable legal representation they need to make sure their assets are passed on to their loved ones when they pass away.

Our will lawyers in Dallas and dedicated legal professionals can help you prepare for life's eventualities and other situations that might take your loved ones by surprise by drafting an effective will. With our firm's legal guidance and assistance, you and your loved ones can feel secure in the future knowing your important affairs will be properly handled when you pass away.

Our compassionate Dallas wills lawyer knows how important it is to proactively plan for the future, so please call (972) 945-1610 or contact us online now so we can discuss everything that needs to be included in your will.

What Is a Will?

A last will and testament is probably the most common form of estate planning. It is typically a typewritten document that is signed in front of a Notary Public. Your will states your wishes regarding the disbursement of assets and allows you to: Designate who receives your assets; Designate who will oversee your estate (Executor); Provide for certain life scenarios, like a child preceding you in death; Ensure the court has a record of your last wishes.

While a will establishes how assets are distributed, it typically must go through the probate process in Texas. Probate ensures the court recognizes the will and authorizes the Executor to carry out your instructions. In the Dallas-Fort Worth area, probate can take several months depending on the complexity of the estate.

For many families, a will works best when coordinated with other tools such as a revocable living trust or financial planning strategies. We help you evaluate which structure best fits your needs.

What Happens If I Pass Away Without A Will?

If you pass away without a valid will, you risk your estate being contested by other parties. If this occurs, your intended beneficiaries will have to go to court to fight for the assets and property that they are entitled to. The best way to avoid unintended consequences with important estate matters is to hire an experienced lawyer to draft your last will and testament.

When Should I Write My Will?

Creating a will should be proactive, not reactive. You should get started on writing your will if you are married, have kids, or have a positive net worth.

Starting a will is often best tackled earlier than most people realize. Life changes rapidly, and ensuring your will reflects your current circumstances can protect assets and dictate their distribution accurately. For young adults, especially those embarking on significant life changes such as buying a home, starting a family, or launching a business, a will ensures that your assets and personal wishes are respected and followed during unforeseen events.

How to Create a Will?

1. Determine the Appropriate Will Type. 2. Outline Your Assets. 3. Designate Beneficiaries. 4. Select an Executor. 5. Address Guardianship for Minors. 6. Consider Charitable Contributions. 7. Witness and Sign. 8. Secure Storage. 9. Regular Review and Updates.

Creating a legally sound will involves thorough preparation and understanding of the legalities involved. Each step in drafting a will ensures that all necessary details regarding your assets and last wishes are meticulously documented. For instance, beneficiaries should be informed of their status in your will to avoid surprises that could lead to conflicts. Additionally, reviewing your will with the assistance of a lawyer, especially after significant life events, helps in making necessary amendments reflecting your current wishes.

What Are the Benefits of a Will?

Writing a will is an essential step in ensuring that your assets are distributed according to your wishes after you pass away. Our experienced wills attorneys at Crain & Wooley can help you create a comprehensive will that covers all aspects of your estate, including property, finances, and guardianship for minor children.

Benefits of having a comprehensive will include: Peace of mind knowing your loved ones are taken care of; Clear instructions for asset distribution; Minimization of potential conflicts among family members; Protection of your children's future; Avoidance of intestacy laws determining asset distribution.

A will also grants you control over who gets what and aids in the efficient management of your affairs. It can also reduce the burden on your loved ones during an emotional time by providing clarity and legal direction. Furthermore, through a will, you can appoint trusted individuals to manage your estate, ensuring your legacy is protected, and family dynamics are preserved. By pre-planning with a will, you're empowering your family by relieving them from making tough decisions on asset and debt distributions during the probate process.

Do not wait until it is too late. Contact us today to start the process of drafting your will and protecting your loved ones' future.

What Are The Requirements For Creating a Valid Texas Will?

Creating a valid will in the state of Texas is crucial for ensuring that your wishes are honored and your assets are distributed according to your intentions. As experienced Dallas-Fort Worth will lawyers, we can guide you through the process to ensure that your will meets all legal requirements.

Key requirements: Age and Capacity - the testator must be at least 18 years old and possess testamentary capacity. Written Document - Texas does not accept oral wills. Proper Signature - the testator must sign the will with two witnesses present. Witnesses - at least two credible witnesses who are at least 14 years old, who should not be beneficiaries. Self-Proving Affidavit - not necessary, but can make the probate process easier.

Ensuring the validity of your will is a fundamental step in safeguarding your estate. By understanding and adhering to state-specific legal guidelines, you minimize the risk of disputes or invalidations. Engaging a knowledgeable attorney to review the legal nuances further ensures the smooth administration of your estate.

Our team of will attorneys in Dallas-Fort Worth can help ensure that your will complies with all Texas laws and effectively reflects your wishes.

Understanding Fort Worth Probate Court

If you are dealing with the probate process in Fort Worth, it is essential to understand how the local probate courts operate. The Tarrant County Probate Court handles most probate matters within the Fort Worth area. Tasks often begin with filing the necessary paperwork, which confirms the validity of the will and designates the executor who will manage the estate's affairs. Estate administration in Fort Worth entails various legal requirements, such as notices to creditors and the inventory of estate assets.

Being aware of the specific procedural requirements and deadlines is crucial. Fort Worth has its own set of local rules that can impact the probate timeline. Working with a Fort Worth-based attorney from Crain & Wooley ensures that all local procedures are accurately followed. Our team is familiar with the intricacies of the Tarrant County Probate Court and can streamline this often complex process.

Choosing the Right Will Attorney in Dallas-Fort Worth

Selecting the right legal partner to prepare your will is a major decision, because you are trusting someone with deeply personal information and long-term plans for your family. When you compare options in the Dallas-Fort Worth area, it helps to look beyond basic credentials and understand how a firm actually works with clients day to day.

As you evaluate a will attorney in Dallas, consider how the firm approaches communication, pricing, and ongoing support. You may want to ask whether they offer flat-rate fees for planning work, what is included in those fees, and how quickly you can expect a response. It can also be useful to learn whether the firm has familiarity with local probate courts such as Tarrant County Probate Court and Dallas County Probate Court.

Many families also benefit from choosing a firm that can stay with them over time as circumstances change. Life events such as moves between Dallas and Fort Worth, new business ventures, or changes in Texas law can all affect the way your will should be structured.

The Role of a Will Lawyer in Fort Worth

Choosing a will lawyer near you in Fort Worth brings numerous advantages in terms of accessibility and personalized service. A local will lawyer is not only familiar with Texas state laws but also understands the nuances of regional requirements and common issues specific to Fort Worth.

Engaging a local attorney means easier consultations and the opportunity for face-to-face meetings. With our client-centric approach, Crain & Wooley prioritizes clear communication and transparency.

Commonly Asked Questions About Wills

What happens if I die without a will? If you pass away without a valid will, your estate may be contested by other parties, leading to potential court battles. It is best to hire an experienced lawyer to draft your last will and testament.

How Does a Will Affect Estate Taxes? A comprehensive will outlines not only the distribution of assets but also helps to strategize around potential estate taxes. In Fort Worth, as part of Texas, there is no state-level estate tax, but federal estate taxes may apply if the estate's value exceeds the federal threshold. Including tax planning strategies within a will can mean setting up trusts that minimize the taxable estate or allocating assets in a way that leverages marital deductions.

When should I update my will? You should update your will when significant life events occur, such as marriage, divorce, birth of a child, or acquisition of new assets.

What Are DIY Wills and Their Risks? DIY wills come with significant risks, particularly if they do not comply with state-specific legal requirements, which can render them invalid. In Fort Worth, a DIY will may overlook critical elements such as witness signatures or specific bequests. Working with a qualified will lawyer ensures compliance with Texas laws.

Can I appoint a guardian for my minor children in my will? Yes, you can designate a guardian for your minor children in your will.

How Secure Is My Will Once Executed? It is essential that the original will is stored in a safe, secure location, such as a safety deposit box or with your attorney. Discussing the storage location with trusted family members or your designated executor can prevent confusion.

Contact Us to Get Started Drafting Your Will

At Crain & Wooley, we understand that you want to feel confident knowing your valuable assets and property will be passed on to your loved ones, which is why we are here to help you draft a will that is tailored to your unique estate planning goals.

If you have additional questions regarding drafting a will, then please get in touch with a will attorney at our firm. When you choose our law firm, we will take the time to get to know you and become familiar with the specific details of your circumstances so that we can devise a customized legal strategy.

/estate-planning/wills/living-wills.body:
Advance directives are legal documents that allow you to make important decisions about your medical care should you become unable to communicate your wishes. There are two types of advance directives: a living will and a durable power of attorney for healthcare. The former allows you to specify the medical procedures you would or would not like to receive in certain situations, while the latter appoints someone you trust to make medical decisions on your behalf.

Advance directives are crucial legal tools empowering individuals to retain control over their healthcare decisions even when incapacitated. They provide a clear roadmap for medical professionals and family members during critical times, avoiding conflicts or uncertainties. Crain & Wooley ensures these documents comply with Texas laws and are tailored to reflect your most current and comprehensive medical directives.

Do I Need An Advance Directive or a Living Will?

Advance directives are important for anyone who wants to take control of what happens to them when they are unable to speak for themselves. They also provide peace of mind for family members who may be unsure of what to do in a medical crisis. Our team of advance directive attorneys in Fort Worth can help you create an advance directive that reflects your wishes, whether you live in Dallas, Plano, or anywhere else in the greater Dallas area.

What Are The Benefits of a Living Will?

A living will is a document that outlines your wishes regarding medical care in certain situations. It can be useful in various circumstances, including when you are terminally ill, in a coma, or otherwise unable to communicate your wishes. A living will can also help you avoid unwanted medical treatment and reduce the burden on your family members who may be unsure of what to do.

Having a living will is particularly essential in today's evolving healthcare environment. It ensures that your ethical, religious, and personal preferences are honored even in situations where you cannot vocalize these preferences. Moreover, it serves as a testament to your foresight in easing the emotional and moral burden on your loved ones during challenging times.

In a living will, you can specify the medical procedures you would or would not like to receive in certain situations. This includes situations such as when you are terminally ill, unconscious, or in a vegetative state. You can also state whether you would like to be taken off life support if it cannot be reversed. Additionally, you can choose to donate your organs or body to science. Your living will should be updated regularly to ensure that it reflects your current wishes.

Durable Power of Attorney for Healthcare in Fort Worth

A durable power of attorney for healthcare allows you to appoint someone you trust to make important medical decisions on your behalf if you are unable to speak for yourself. It is especially important to have a durable power of attorney for healthcare if you have a serious medical condition, such as a terminal illness, or if you are elderly. If you do not have a durable power of attorney for healthcare, a judge may have to appoint a guardian to make medical decisions for you.

A durable power of attorney for healthcare allows you to choose the person you trust most to make important medical decisions on your behalf. This person is known as the agent. The agent can make medical decisions for you if you are unable to communicate your wishes, such as whether to take you off life support if it cannot be reversed or to donate your organs or body to science. The durable power of attorney for healthcare can also name a successor agent if the person you originally chose is unable to serve.

How To Choose An Agent

Choosing an agent through a durable power of attorney for healthcare is not just about naming someone; it involves selecting an individual who understands your values and is committed to respecting your wishes. It's critical to have candid discussions with your chosen agent about your medical preferences and values. This robust tool ensures that someone aligned with your views is legally empowered to make real-time critical decisions.

How to Create an Advance Directive

Creating an advance directive is a simple process you can complete with the help of an experienced attorney. Our team at Crain & Wooley can help you create a living will or a durable power of attorney for healthcare that accurately reflects your wishes. We can also help you update your advance directive as your situation or wishes change.

The creation of an advance directive involves not just filling out forms but engaging in meaningful one-on-one consultations to ensure every detail is addressed. During these sessions, we delve deep into our clients' concerns and specific needs, offering tailored advice and practical solutions to match their unique circumstances.

Understanding Texas Advance Directive Laws

In Texas, the laws surrounding advance directives stipulate specific criteria that must be met for these documents to be legally binding. It's essential that these documents are signed by the principal and witnessed by two disinterested witnesses, neither of whom should be related by blood or marriage. This ensures the directives are carried out according to the individual's authentic decisions, free of undue influence. Additionally, understanding the distinctions between a living will and a medical power of attorney is important for compliance and execution.

Texas law has specific guidelines for caregivers and medical facilities in handling advance directives. It's recommended to store these documents where they can be easily accessed, especially during emergency medical situations. At Crain & Wooley, we advise clients on proper storage options and how to keep loved ones informed of their healthcare wishes.

FAQ About Advance Directives or Living Wills in Fort Worth

What Is the Difference Between a Living Will and a Durable Power of Attorney for Healthcare? A living will documents your specific wishes regarding medical treatments under certain conditions. Conversely, a durable power of attorney for healthcare appoints someone—referred to as your 'agent'—to make healthcare decisions for you if you are incapacitated.

Why Are Advance Directives Important in Medical Emergencies? Advance directives hold paramount importance during medical emergencies as they communicate your healthcare preferences clearly when you cannot. They dictate the course of medical actions rather than leaving decisions to relatives who may be uninformed about your wishes.

How Often Should You Update Your Advance Directive? It is advisable to review and update your advance directive regularly. Life events such as marriage, divorce, the diagnosis of a significant medical condition, or milestone birthdays are opportune times to revisit your directives.

Can My Advance Directive Be Changed After It's Created? Yes, you can modify, update, or revoke your advance directive at any time, provided you are still competent to make decisions. Changes should be documented in writing, signed, and dated.

Who Should Have a Copy of My Advance Directive? Key individuals include your assigned healthcare agent, primary care physician, attorney, and family members. Keeping copies with these people ensures that in any critical medical situation, your preferences are immediately accessible and adhered to.
